title: Address at the World Economic Forum Annual Meeting
titleOriginal: Discurso en el Foro Económico Mundial

paragraphs.text: Good afternoon. Thank you for the invitation. I am here today to tell you that the Western world is in danger. It is in danger because those who are supposed to defend the values of the West are co-opted by a vision of the world that inevitably leads to socialism and thereby to poverty.

Unfortunately, in recent decades, motivated by some well-meaning individuals and others who wish to belong to a privileged caste, the main leaders of the Western world have abandoned the model of freedom for different versions of what we call collectivism. We are here to tell you that collectivist experiments are never the solution. They are the problem.

Let me explain why. If we look at the history of economic progress, we see an undeniable fact. From the year zero of the Christian era to approximately 1800, world per capita GDP remained essentially constant. Life for the average human being was nasty, brutish, and short, to borrow the words of Thomas Hobbes. Then, something extraordinary happened. Capitalism arrived.

With the introduction of capitalism as an economic system, free trade, and the defense of private property, the world experienced a transformation unlike anything in human history. Per capita GDP multiplied over fifteen times. This was not an accident. This was the direct, irrefutable result of free markets, free enterprise, and the limitation of state power.

If we look at the data from the year 1800 to 2000, we find that capitalism lifted 90 percent of the world's population out of extreme poverty. That is a fact, not an opinion. The free market is the most powerful engine of prosperity the world has ever known. And yet, there are those in this very room who would dismantle it. Who would replace it with regulation, redistribution, and central planning.

I know that to many here, I am saying something uncomfortable. But that is precisely why I am here. Someone must say it. The problem with the West is not capitalism. The problem with the West is that capitalism is under attack. It is under attack from those who would use the state to redistribute wealth by force, destroying incentives and innovation in the process.

The state is not the solution. The state is the problem. When the state intervenes in the economy, it creates distortions. It creates privileges for some at the expense of others. It generates corruption, inefficiency, and dependency. Every peso that the state takes from a productive citizen and gives to an unproductive use is a peso that destroys value.

I come from Argentina, a country that was once among the richest in the world. In 1895, Argentina's per capita income was higher than that of Germany, France, and Italy. What happened? A century of collectivism happened. A century of protectionism, state intervention, fiscal irresponsibility, and the printing of money. My country went from being a beacon of prosperity to suffering chronic inflation, poverty, and decline.

Argentina is a cautionary tale for the entire West. It shows you what happens when politicians promise people things that can only be delivered through confiscation and redistribution. It shows you what happens when the state grows without limit. It shows you the endgame of socialism in all its variants. And I am here to say: we are reversing course.

Now let me address the issue of so-called social justice. Social justice is not just. It is, in fact, inherently unjust. Because it treats people not as individuals with equal rights but as members of groups with special claims. It replaces merit with privilege. It replaces equality before the law with equality of outcomes imposed by force.

The concept of market failure is one of the great myths used to justify state intervention. Behind every supposed market failure, if you look closely, you will find a government failure. Regulations that distort incentives. Taxes that crush enterprise. Subsidies that create moral hazard. The solution to government-created problems is never more government.

Let me be even more direct. The agenda being pushed in many international forums today, under the banner of sustainability, equity, and social responsibility, is nothing more than socialism dressed in new clothes. The language changes, but the mechanism is the same: use state power to control individuals, confiscate their property, and dictate how they must live.

To the entrepreneurs and business leaders in this room, I say: do not be intimidated by the collectivist agenda. Do not be ashamed of your success. Do not apologize for creating wealth. You are not the villains. You are the heroes. Every business you build, every job you create, every product you bring to market, improves the lives of your fellow human beings.

Capitalism is not just the best system we have ever tried. It is the only moral system. Because it is the only system that respects individual liberty. The only system based on voluntary exchange. The only system in which success comes from serving others, not from expropriating them. Free markets are not perfect, but they are infinitely superior to any alternative devised by the human mind.

In Argentina, we have begun the most ambitious reform program in our history. We are cutting government spending. We are deregulating the economy. We are opening markets. We are eliminating ministries that serve no purpose other than employing political allies. The path is difficult, but the direction is clear: more freedom, less state.

I want to end with a message of hope and a warning. The message of hope is that the ideas of liberty are alive. People are waking up to the failure of collectivism. A new generation is discovering the works of Ludwig von Mises, Friedrich Hayek, and Murray Rothbard. The warning is this: if the leaders of the West do not change course, if they continue down the path of regulation, taxation, and state expansion, the West will decline. The choice is clear: freedom or serfdom. Long live freedom, dammit. Thank you.
